speaker: interviewer
text: Date is September 13, 1995. Survivor is Marion Horn, last name at birth, Loew. Interviewer, Rivka Wakslak. Place is Long Beach, New York, in USA. Language is English. You don't want me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's right. You know what?

speaker: interviewer
text: I have no mic on. Is that OK? This is Marion Horn. I am Rivka Wakslak. Today is September 13, 1995. We'll be conducting this interview in English. Mrs. Horn, thank you so much for participating in the project. Can you please tell me your full name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marion Loew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-E-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have-- did you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at birth. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 19th, 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address where you lived when your parents-- when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, my parents lived on 9 Elisabethstrasse in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Irma. And my father's name was Solomon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Loew. Loew, [? 'Luhv' ?] in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Um, did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one brother, Edgar. And he was born in 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family. What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the very, very fine linen business, both manufacturing and selling. And he dealt with some of the finest stores throughout the world. And consequently, he did quite a bit traveling, uh, and had connections and friends all over the world. Uh, my mother was a homemaker, but not in the true sense of the American homemaker. Um, it was a different lifestyle and a different time. There were help in the house. But she managed a very large household. Um, give you an idea of the kind of household-- my parents had a dining room with a dining room table that could accommodate comfortably 48 people. So it was-- everything was large. And when she went marketing, it was for 100 pounds of flour and 100 pounds of sugar and 100 pounds of coffee, the sort of-- that kind of homemaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: They entertained often?

speaker: interviewee
text: They entertained often and a lot. Friday night, my father used to come home. We never knew how many people came home with him from synagogue. Whoever was passing through and didn't have a place to go came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which synagogue did your father go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a conservative synagogue-- uh, not an orthodox, but a conservative synagogue. And he was very active, both in the temple and in the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents lived a very comfortable life in Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet they left Munich sh-- soon after you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left Munich just about when Hitler came into power.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what prompted them to leave Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I guess my father's foresight, anticipating things to come. He wanted to come to the United States and had applied for visa. He had the opportunity. He had relatives in the United States-- very well-to-do relatives, distant cousins who established themselves in the 1800s and had mills in the South, in North Carolina. And-- but there was no way of getting into the United States. Kept applying and reapplying and was told that there is no, uh, quota. The quota was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he did-- he had dealt a great deal with Switzerland, and we went to Switzerland. However, Switzerland did not accept any foreigners to become citizens. And-- but we were able to live comfortably in Switzerland because we were not going to become burdened on the Swiss government. And you had to almost reassure them that. Uh, it was only later on that people were able-- after the war-- to become citizens of Switzerland if they needed you for a particular trade or a profession that they were short in manpower. So we moved to Switzerland. Where in Switzerland did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lugano.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lived in Lugano.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address in Lugano?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were living in Suvigliana, which is a small town, one stop on the funicular on Monte Bre. And absolutely most magnificent spot of our-- that one can imagine. Italian part of Switzerland, so consequently I grew up with Italian and German and, um, French being the third language. We also traveled extensively to Yugoslavia, where my father had a factory and where many of the items were manufactured and embroidered-- all hand-embroidered. And we had a townhouse in Zagreb and a farm outside of Zagreb, where we, as children, spent very happy times. Zagreb was a very cosmopolitan city at one time, with a large population also of Jewish people, mixture of Hungarian, Austrian, German, Turkish, very interesting mixture of-- of people. And as a matter of fact, uh, the house that we had in Zagreb, which was a townhouse, was later on taken over by the Gestapo as their headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Zagreb.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they used the better buildings for their quarters. Um, we used to go back and forth. Uh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you travel between Lugano and Zagreb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, every summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a year, you would go to Zagreb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. We would go to Zagreb and to the ocean, to the, uh, you know, to the, uh, resorts in Yugoslavia, which were beautiful in the Adriatic-- some close to Italy and some further south on the Adriatic coast. Um, just very nice time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in Lugano and Zagreb for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was the best of both worlds.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how many years did you live this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the time that I was two until about seven, eight, eight, eight or nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you started school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started school. I started elementary school. And then in 1939, we finally received the OK that, you know, we could come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about life in-- in Lugano as a small child. What do you remember? Do you have any friends? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had lots of friends. I had lots of friends. I had lots of animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dogs, cats, birds, rabbits, tortoises, you name it. Um, we had two parrots that my brother taught every vile curse word in the Italian language that we had to hide when businesspeople came to the-- to the house to visit my father because it was abominable, the language the birds had. [LAUGHS] So it was-- [LAUGHS] it was-- it was fun. It was fun times. Um, it was also-- I was extremely close to my father, so once a year he used to take one of us on a business trip with him. And that's probably some of the best memories that I have of my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what a business trip was like when you went with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Business trip was going to Paris [LAUGHS] or going to Athens, and, you know, being a little girl and being treated like royalty by my father and by the people around him, and being treated like an adult, and just having wonderful, wonderful time. Um, just very rewarding, just being with him. And also, my father was a opera buff, so we had Milano not very far from us. And I can almost say that I grew up at La Scala because when I was this high, I used to go to the opera with my father and mother. But very often, it was just the two of us. And we used to go through the libretto. And it's-- I'm amazed because when I think of it as a young child, you would think that you would be bored by it. But I was never bored. I loved it. And I think I just loved being with him, and the enthusiasm that he had just rubbed off. And now I'm an opera buff, [LAUGHS] so and it all comes back. This is like old friends. And it's part of-- sort of important part of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- he-- liked to collect art.  He liked-- he was an art enthusiast?

speaker: interviewee
text: He collected everything and anything that was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paintings, crystal, rare books, stamps, carpets, silver, Judaic silver artifacts-- just very much involved in all the arts. And he was a collector.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any favorite--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a lot of-- we had a lot of art in the house, and very fine art and magnificent paintings.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were van Dycks, van 'Deeks.' He had a small Rembrandt, um, a Rubens. And this was all-- but he-- he enjoyed the acquisition. He knew what he was buying at the time. And whatever he did was quality. Um, he was strict in a sense, but very, very mild-mannered. I don't think I ever received a harsh word from my father, which was not the same with my mother. [LAUGHS] She was the very strict disciplinarian and believed that children should be seen and not heard at all. Um, but that's, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you must have a large house to accommodate all this art.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large? How many rooms did you have in Lugano?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say probably around 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a large staff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a cook, who was a orphan. And my parents made a wedding for her and a trousseau, like she was their own daughter. And she was part of the household. Then there was a baby nurse that was there for many, many years, who was a niece of I'm not sure whether it was Goebbels or Goring and who was with the house-- with-- with-- with us for probably about seven or eight years. She arrived on the scene before I was born. And the poor woman was never allowed to go on vacation because I just cried the first time she went away. So the second time, she said, OK, fine. I'll take you. [LAUGHS] And I carried on so that it was hopeless. She couldn't go anywhere. I loved her very much and she was-- uh, she was a wonderful woman. She was a buffer between my mother and myself. My mother was very, very strict and extremely critical of me. And she was the buffer. So without her, I felt like I was lost. And so I never let her out of my sight. And then, of course, the day came where all good things have come to an end. We, uh, were coming to, uh-- as I said, we finally received visa to come to the United States. And we were in-- at that time, it was called Abbazia, Italy. And all our belongings were at the port in Trieste. Um-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on, um--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were supposed to leave. It was on what they call lifts in those days. And, well, the ship never left. Uh, Poland was being invaded, Czechoslovakia, Austria. And we officially had left Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had an exit visa from Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an exit visa from Switzerland, but no reentry visa from Switzerland. We also did not have-- and I-- I don't know how or-- but we did not have an entry visa to Yugoslavia. Uh, as I mentioned earlier, my father had connections through business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- you-- the boat never left. You were heading--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never left.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never left from Trieste.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the boat never left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everything the family owns is on the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to everything that was on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the end of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the end of it. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family's now left in Trieste with no place to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're citizens without citizenship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was at the end of '39. '39-- I am not completely clear with the chronology of it because unfortunately, I don't have anybody to lean on in that respect. Um, the only thing I can rely on is my own memory. And after the war, my parents and brother and I, we very, very rarely had any conversations about what had occurred during that period. I think everybody just wanted to put it behind and get on with the present. And the present was very difficult when we finally did come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you're in Trieste.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we're in Trieste. And my father said, well-- there might-- there was the possibility that in a couple weeks, the boat might be able to leave. So my father said, well, look. You stay here-- and because we had a visa in Italy for a certain period of time and the visa had not expired-- um, I'm going to Yugoslavia and tie up some loose ends. And I'll come back and join you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said-- my brother and I-- we said no. If you're going, we're all going. These are turbulent times and we don't want to leave you out of our sight. So we all went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And going back to Yugoslavia was very, very difficult. Um, we could not get back into Yugoslavia. So my parents had to make arrangements with people that were going to take us by boat at night across the border. And you had to get far enough out onto the ocean not to be within the lighthouse-- rays of the lighthouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, OK, we are going by boat-- my brother, my father, and my mother-- and the little girl can just walk over the bridge. And this was from Rijeka, which is now-- it was called Fiume at the time. Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-I-U-M-E. But now it's Rijeka. They renamed many of the, uh-- of the cities. Into the Italian little-- this was Italy at one time. Now it's belongs to Yugoslavia. It's, you know, the area that went back and forth. To a little fishing village across the bridge called Susak, S-U-S with a hook, A-K. And they had to-- we had to wait for an evening where there was no moon. So they went. And I walked with a stranger across the bridge. And I was taken to some people with my little doll that I will get back to you later on. I had a little-- little Raggedy Ann doll. And a horrendous storm broke out. But it really-- a hurricane level type of storm. And my parents did not show up. Hours and hours and hours went by. And I was with these people. And they started to look over of what possessions I had because they were convinced that my-- that they had drowned at sea. And that was a very frightening experience because here I was, completely alone, without-- nothing, strangers. I had a little, tiny little ring with a sapphire and a little gold necklace with a little [INAUDIBLE] and my name on it, a round plaque. And they were eyeing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then towards morning when they didn't show up, they said, what should we do with her? They didn't realize that I understood and spoke Yugoslav. We can't-- what are we going to do with her? So one of the-- I had an uncle at that time who was staying in Rijeka, in Fiume. He had just been released from Dachau. He was in Dachau for about six month, and he was released. This was your father's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One of his younger brothers. And he was a bachelor. So I said, I have an uncle in Fiume. Could you take me back? So they said OK. They took my ring off. They took my little chain gold plaque off. And they said, OK, fine. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met my uncle-- who I really had very little contact with over the years-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not knowing whether I was an orphan, what, how, where. And two days had gone by. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were probably one of-- some of the most traumatic days in my life. Um, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had a whistle that my father had, a certain whistle that was our-- and I-- I guess I must have fallen asleep and I heard the whistle. I thought I was dreaming. And then I heard it again. That was my father. The boat smashed against the rocks and they were able to crawl along the ocean into a cave and save themselves. And then gradually, it took them a couple days to make their way back. And my father assumed that I would be with my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: He hadn't given you instructions prior? They hadn't discussed. They just assumed it would go well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody anticipated the storm. We didn't have weather-- weather people at that time to forecast. I guess the fishermen might have known, but we didn't. And then they waited about two or three days. And we went off on the expedition all over again. And at that time, it worked. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we made it into Yugoslavia. And then I went to school for a couple months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Yugoslavia did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Samobor, at the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the farm. And was that your own farm that you had from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. And there were people there who were taking care of it.  And my father had told them that if he doesn't come back, they should just take the property. And, you know, they were with him for years and years. And I think the farm was essentially there to-- really, for us children. And, uh, these people who ran it, he said, you know, it's yours. Whatever you harvest, it's yours. Sell it, you know, whatever you want to do with it. And, uh-- but we-- you know, my brother and I-- we enjoyed the cows and the animals. And, uh, it was just a very rewarding experience to be with the nature and the farm animals and the chickens. And totally different contrast, like day and night, from our formal existence otherwise. There, we felt free. We could wear lederhosens all the time. We didn't have to get dressed. And it was just a very, very relaxed portion of our childhood. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Samobor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samobor, I think it was a couple of-- a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything was in chaos at that point around us. Um, we didn't know what was happening. And I certainly at that time didn't know exactly what was happening to Yugoslavia. Uh, to me, it was like a safe haven, um, in terms of the war. Even though I was a precocious child, I was not told the details of exactly-- I knew the different countries that had fallen to Hitler, but not, you know, not completely aware.
